I believe with everything you're one of the most loyal honest passionate individual that believes in doing right and I loved it you're passionate and enjoy helping people by educating your followers with the knowledge you hold and asking nothing in return educating for free.... I hope you get this comment and reach out to me then let me know what you think about what I've said.... my name is Julian Powell but you can call me JP.. PS this could be a stupid idea.. it's better to take the chance end it being a failure then a possible success left unsaid never developed...

author: @timmyssweet
text: If you increased or decreased the size of the gears, the timing would change.  Similarly, if you used belts and increased or decreased the size of the cog, the timing or speed would change. If you could use both polarities of a motor, there would be no need to keep the same rotation.  I think EVs have done that for t their break charging.  Actually, electronics made most gears systems redundant.

author: @danblankenship5744
text:
I love the idea!
That’s actually the second big issue I’ve been working on in my own bicycle design.
Mine is powered by body weight and downward pressure from the legs. It works great once you're up to speed and riding on flat terrain.
I’ve been thinking that adding a throttle-controlled CVT could help compensate for hills and varying loads—allowing smoother performance overall.
One of the biggest challenges in my design is that it requires a full up-and-down stroke to operate. So, starting from a mid-stroke position becomes a major problem.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I believe elliptical chainsets were tried for exactly that problem mate - I don't know what became of them though

replies.author: @danblankenship5744
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering
I saw a chainless, crankless, lever-driven bicycle called the NuBike. It’s somewhat similar, but instead of giving each foot its own lever, both feet rest on the same lever during the downstroke. You stand to push it down, and at the bottom of the stroke, you sit on a seat that’s connected to the other lever. This setup lets you alternate between standing and sitting, using your body weight to drive each stroke.
Stand up, sit down—repeat as needed.

If the pivot point or the length of the lever could be made adjustable, you could vary the torque output dynamically. That might be a good way to handle heavy loads or uphill climbs.
Plus, making the handle bars wrap further back along your sides, you could use your upper body strength to share the load.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @FuhrChris
text:
You need to make a double ram version.
Create two straight arms that are in opposite Orientation's  One is up while the other is down.  
Yet the rotation of the gears remains the same.
This would form the basis of a set of bike pedals in which you stand on first the left straight arm and then switch to standing on the right straight arm.  
This the waisted energy of rotating pedals is removed from the equations.

author: @donaldhoudek2889
text: Peacocks, when I went to my grand parents farm in South West Michigan, my friend's parents over there used to raise and sell Peacocks and Parakeets.  Every now and then a Peacock would manage to get of the pen and we had to chase it down catch it and put it back. Let me tell you, those birds were beautiful, but VERY vicious, I happened to find out the hard way. Parakeets were OK, but a little noisy. Another Outstanding video, thank you Robert, now to crank up the 3D printer.

author: @1992jamo
text:
I was talking to my Dad on the phone last night, quite enthusiastically about your previous video and how it can work as a constantly variable clutch, who's first reaction after being quite interested was "There is probably something tangentially relevant buried in a Victorian engineering book that has been long forgotten.". Apparently great minds think alike.
You have motivated me enough to give my 3D printer a proper clean.
